stem: What is the sum of all positive divisors of \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \)?
solution: For \( n = p^{a}q^{b} \), the sum of divisors factors as \( \sigma(n) = \frac{p^{a+1}-1}{p-1}\cdot\frac{q^{b+1}-1}{q-1} \). Substituting gives \( \sigma([[nval]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( n \) itself, not the sum of all its divisors.
This is the number of divisors \( (a+1)(b+1) \), not their sum.
This is the sum of the proper divisors, which omits \( n \) itself.
This adds the two top prime-power terms but drops the rest of each geometric series.
This is one more than the correct sum.
This is one less than the correct sum.
This is two more than the correct sum.

stem: What is the sum of the PROPER positive divisors of \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \) (that is, all positive divisors except \( [[nval]] \) itself)?
solution: The sum of ALL divisors is \( \sigma(n)=\frac{p^{a+1}-1}{p-1}\cdot\frac{q^{b+1}-1}{q-1} \); subtracting \( n \) itself leaves only the proper divisors: \( \sigma([[nval]]) - [[nval]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \sigma(n) \), the sum of ALL divisors, forgetting to exclude \( n \) itself.
This is \( n \) itself, not the sum of its proper divisors.
This is one less than the divisor COUNT, not the divisor sum.
This adds \( n \) back on top of the correct proper-divisor sum.
This is one more than the correct sum.
This is one less than the correct sum.
This is two more than the correct sum.

stem: What is the sum of all positive divisors of the number \( [[nval]] \)?
distractor_rationales: This is \( n \) itself, not the sum of all its divisors.
This is the number of divisors, not their sum.
This just adds the two prime factors, ignoring the exponents and the geometric series.
This is one more than the correct sum.
This is one less than the correct sum.
This is two more than the correct sum.

stem: Let \( n = [[nval]] = [[p]] \cdot [[q]] \) for distinct primes \( [[p]] \) and \( [[q]] \). What is the sum of all positive divisors of \( n^{2} = [[n2val]] \)?
solution: Squaring \( n \) doubles both exponents: \( n^{2} = [[p]]^{2}[[q]]^{2} \), so \( \sigma(n^{2}) = \frac{p^{3}-1}{p-1}\cdot\frac{q^{3}-1}{q-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \sigma(n) \), the sum of divisors of \( n \) itself, forgetting that squaring doubles the exponents.
This is \( n^{2} \) itself, not the sum of its divisors.
This uses exponent 1 instead of 2 in each geometric-series numerator, i.e. computes \( \sigma(n) \) again.
This is one more than the correct sum.
This is one less than the correct sum.
This is two more than the correct sum.

stem: What is the sum of all positive divisors of \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \cdot [[r]]^{[[c]]} \)?
solution: For \( n = p^{a}q^{b}r^{c} \) with three distinct primes, the sum-of-divisors function multiplies across all three: \( \sigma(n) = \frac{p^{a+1}-1}{p-1}\cdot\frac{q^{b+1}-1}{q-1}\cdot\frac{r^{c+1}-1}{r-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( n \) itself, not the sum of all its divisors.
This is the divisor COUNT, not the divisor SUM.
This drops the third prime's geometric series entirely, using only two of the three factors.
This adds the three top prime-power terms but drops the rest of each geometric series.
This is one more than the correct sum.
This is one less than the correct sum.
This is two more than the correct sum.

stem: Let \( n = [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \). What is the sum of the positive divisors of \( n \) that are divisible by \( [[q]] \)?
solution: A divisor of \( n \) divisible by \( [[q]] \) has the form \( p^{i}q^{k} \) with \( 0\le i\le[[a]] \) and \( 1\le k\le[[b]] \). Summing over \( i \) gives the full geometric series \( \frac{p^{a+1}-1}{p-1} \), while summing \( q^{k} \) for \( k=1,\dots,[[b]] \) gives \( \frac{q([[q]]^{[[b]]}-1)}{q-1} \). Multiplying these gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \sigma(n) \), the sum of ALL divisors, forgetting to restrict to multiples of \( [[q]] \).
This is \( n \) itself, not a sum of divisors.
This multiplies exponent-based counts instead of summing the actual divisor values.
This starts the \( q \)-power series at exponent 0 instead of 1, missing the required factor of \( [[q]] \).
This is one more than the correct sum.
This is one less than the correct sum.
This is two more than the correct sum.
